questions.question: Explain the charAt() method with an example. What exception is thrown and when?
keyPoints: Method signature: char charAt(int index)
Returns character at specified index
Throws StringIndexOutOfBoundsException if index < 0 or >= length()
Example: "Hello".charAt(1) returns 'e'
Index range is 0 to length()-1

questions.question: What is the difference between getChars() and toCharArray() methods in Java?
keyPoints: getChars() copies to existing char array
toCharArray() creates and returns new char array
getChars parameters: srcBegin, srcEnd, dst[], dstBegin
toCharArray requires no parameters
getChars is more memory efficient for large strings

questions.question: Write a Java method to count the occurrences of a character in a string without using built-in methods.
keyPoints: Use charAt() in a loop from 0 to length()-1
Compare each character with target using ==
Increment counter on match
Handle null/empty string edge cases
Time complexity: O(n) where n is string length

questions.question: Explain Unicode support in Java strings. What is the Basic Multilingual Plane?
keyPoints: Java uses UTF-16 encoding for strings
Each char is 16-bit (2 bytes)
BMP covers Unicode from U+0000 to U+FFFF
Characters beyond BMP use surrogate pairs
codePointAt() handles surrogate pairs properly

questions.question: What are the different ways to extract a substring in Java? Explain with examples.
keyPoints: substring(int beginIndex) - from beginIndex to end
substring(int beginIndex, int endIndex) - endIndex exclusive
Both return new String objects
Shorthand: "Hello".substring(1,4) returns "ell"
Can use split() and then take parts as alternative

questions.question: Write a Java program to reverse a string using character extraction methods. Explain your approach and analyze its time and space complexity.
keyPoints: Use charAt() or toCharArray() to access characters
Build reversed string using StringBuilder or character array
Method 1: charAt() in reverse loop, append to StringBuilder
Method 2: toCharArray(), swap characters in place
Time: O(n), Space: O(n) for StringBuilder or O(1) for in-place array swap
Explain why direct string modification isn't possible due to immutability

questions.question: Create a Java method that extracts all unique characters from a string and returns them in the order of their first occurrence. Implement it using character extraction methods.
keyPoints: Use LinkedHashSet to maintain insertion order
Iterate using charAt() from 0 to length()-1
Add each character to Set (duplicates automatically ignored
Alternatively use boolean array for ASCII characters
Time complexity: O(n) with HashSet, O(1) for character lookup
Space complexity: O(n) worst case for all unique characters

questions.question: Explain the immutability of strings in Java. How does this affect character extraction operations? Provide examples showing how extraction methods create new strings rather than modifying the original.
keyPoints: String is immutable - cannot be changed after creation
All character extraction methods (charAt, substring, toCharArray) return new objects
Original string remains unchanged after extraction
Benefits: security, thread-safety, hashcode caching
Example: String s = "Hello"; s.substring(0,3) creates new "Hel" without modifying s
Memory implications: multiple extractions create multiple objects
